walkthrough.title: Place the Gameboard
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Boonlake set up. First, unfold the main gameboard and place it in the center of the table. This is a big board showing the lake region with four named areas — Boonlake, Southern, New Hope, and Unknown — connected by bridges. You will also see a winding river along the right side with spaces for ships, locks for interim scoring, and a fork that splits into two arms.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the 42 building tiles. Sort them by the symbols on their backs. Set aside the 7 cattle pasture tiles — these are double-sided with pastures on both sides. Put them in a stack next to the gameboard. Then take the building tiles with gray backs, shuffle them face down, and form a mound next to the board. These are the tiles you will reveal when exploring.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now place building tiles on the gameboard based on player count. For 3 players, mix the 8 building tiles with the two matching back symbols and place them face up on the 8 matching spaces on the board. Remove the 2 tiles with the fourth-player symbol from the game. For 4 players, use all 10 starting tiles on all 10 matching spaces. For 1 or 2 players, use only the 6 tiles matching that symbol. The tile does not have to go on a space showing its own exact symbol.

walkthrough.title: Place Region Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the 4 region markers. Place one on each of the four region labels on the gameboard — Boonlake, Southern, New Hope, and Unknown. These markers track which region rewards have been claimed during the Region Scoring action. They get reset at each interim scoring.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the following items next to the gameboard as a shared supply: all 62 coins (in denominations of 1, 5, and 20), all 14 vases, and all 36 lever tiles. Make sure everyone can reach them. These are communal resources players will take throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal Project Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 165 project cards and form a face-down draw pile next to the gameboard. Then deal 6 cards to each player. Take them into your hand and keep them secret for now. You will look at them soon when making your starting choices.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Action Board
walkthrough.text: Place the action board next to the gameboard. This is the tall narrow board with rows for the action strips. Shuffle all 7 action strips and place them on the action board in random order, one per row. Each strip shows a unique action — Pioneer, Settle, Cattle-breeding, Region Scoring, Hire, Progress, and Builder. On your turn, you will pull one of these strips to take its action.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Player Board
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one double-layer player board and places it in front of them. Make sure the multiplayer side is face up — the back side is only for the solo game. The board has recesses for houses, settlements, cattle, production sites, and a modernization area with spaces for lever tiles. There are also five tiers of wooden pieces you will remove over the course of the game to unlock income and score points.

walkthrough.title: Load Your Player Board with Wooden Pieces
walkthrough.text: Everyone chooses a color and takes their matching wooden pieces. Place them on your player board as follows: put your 6 houses, 4 settlements, and 5 cattle in the appropriate recesses on the tiered section of your board. These cover income spaces — as you remove them to the gameboard, you unlock income you receive at each interim scoring.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Inhabitants
walkthrough.text: Take your 10 inhabitants in your color. Place 3 of them on the ranch at the bottom left of your player board. Place 2 in the two recesses next to the houses on your board — these are the tier bonus inhabitants that unlock when you clear those tiers. The remaining 5 go into the general supply. You can only get inhabitants from the supply through specific actions like the Hire action.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Ship on the River
walkthrough.text: Take your ship in your color. For a 3 or 4 player game, place it on the third space of the river on the gameboard. For a 1 or 2 player game, it goes on the first space instead. Your ship will advance down the river throughout the game, and its movement triggers interim scoring when it crosses locks.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Boats on the Canal
walkthrough.text: Take your 2 boats and place them both on the canal on your player board, positioned in the area of wood production. These boats are your resource generators — each boat gives you 1 resource matching the type shown next to its current canal position. You can slide boats downstream for free or pay 2 coins to move one upstream.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Markers on the Gameboard Tracks
walkthrough.text: Take your 3 player markers. Place one on the starting space of the victory point track. Place one on the coin income track at the space showing 2 coins. Place one on the card income track at the space showing 2 cards. These markers track your VP, coin income, and card income throughout the game. Also take your 4 scoring markers and your 100/200 marker and set them next to your player board.

walkthrough.title: Draw and Place Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 16 scoring tiles. Each player draws 2. Look at your hand cards and your scoring tiles before deciding. Choose 1 scoring tile and place it in the area matching your color at the bottom of the gameboard — this is your personal scoring tile and earns double points when you score it or complete its special project. Return the other tile. For any unoccupied player areas in a game with fewer than 4 players, place a random scoring tile in each one. Return leftover tiles to the box.

walkthrough.title: Place Your First Production Site
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their 4 production sites — one each of stone, iron, loam, and wood. Choose one and place it level-1 side up in the matching recess at the top left of your player board. This gives you 1 resource of that type whenever you need to show resources for card costs. Tip: look at the resource requirements on your hand cards and your chosen scoring tile to pick the most useful starting resource.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Coins and First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who most recently traveled to a small town gets the starting player token and receives 6 coins. The next player clockwise gets 7 coins. In a 3 or 4 player game, the remaining players each get 8 coins. This staggered starting money compensates for turn order advantage. Now you are ready to play Boonlake!

walkthrough.title: Game Overview: Two Rounds
walkthrough.text: Boonlake is played over exactly 2 rounds. Each round ends when any player moves their ship onto the last river space. Within each round, there are 2 interim scorings triggered when a ship crosses a lock for the first time. So across the whole game, you will have 4 interim scorings total — 2 per round. After the final interim scoring at the end of round 2, there is also a final scoring. The player with the most victory points wins.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Three Phases
walkthrough.text: On each turn, you execute three phases in order. Phase A: choose one action strip from the action board and carry it out. Phase B: advance your ship along the river. Phase C: push the remaining action strips together to close the gap. Then the next player clockwise takes their turn. This three-phase structure repeats until the round ends. Let's dig into each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase A: Choosing an Action Strip
walkthrough.text: Look at the action board. The 7 action strips are stacked vertically. You pick one strip and pull it down to the bottom area of the action board. Here is the important part: if you choose one of the two bottom strips, you must move your VP marker backward by the amount shown for that row. The higher strips are free, but they might not have the action you want. This creates a constant tension between getting the action you need and paying VPs for it.

walkthrough.title: Phase A: Resolving the Action Strip
walkthrough.text: Each action strip has three sections. On the left is a card slot — you may play one project card of the shown type (day, sunset, or night) by paying its cost, or discard any card of that type for 2 coins, or skip entirely. In the middle are the active player's effects — you resolve these left to right. On the right side of the arrow are effects that all players (including you) may carry out. The exception is the Pioneer action, where only the other players get the right-side effect.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Advance Your Ship
walkthrough.text: After your action, you move your ship down the river. The space you pulled the action strip from shows the maximum number of steps your ship may move. You must move at least 1 step. Harbors can hold any number of ships and always count toward movement. Regular spaces hold only 1 ship — if a space is occupied, you skip over it without counting it. You can never go backward. When your ship lands on a space, you collect its reward — coins, cards, inhabitants, vases, VP, or a production site upgrade.

walkthrough.title: Locks and Interim Scoring Triggers
walkthrough.text: There are 3 locks on the river. When you are the first player in the current round to move your ship past a lock, an interim scoring happens immediately after you collect your river reward. If another player already crossed that lock this round, you do not trigger a scoring. In the first round, ships take the upper fork in the river. In the second round, they take the lower fork. At the end of the round, ships reset to their starting positions for round 2.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Close the Gap
walkthrough.text: After your ship moves, push the remaining action strips on the action board upward to close the gap left by the strip you took. Now there is one strip per row again and it is the next player's turn. The strip you took is at the bottom and will be available next turn — but at a potential VP cost since it is in a lower row.

walkthrough.title: Playing Project Cards
walkthrough.text: Many actions let you play a project card. Cards cost a combination of coins, vases, and resources. Coins and vases are paid from your supply. Resources work differently — you show proof of having them through your production sites and boats, but you do not spend them. If your level-2 loam production site gives you 2 loam, you always have 2 loam available for any card that requires it. Your 2 boats each provide 1 resource of whatever type they are sitting next to on the canal. You can move boats downstream for free or pay 2 coins to move one upstream to get a different resource.

walkthrough.title: Card Types and Effects
walkthrough.text: Every project card belongs to one of three types: day, sunset, or night. Some actions restrict which type you can play. Cards have three kinds of effects shown by symbols. A lightning bolt means the effect happens immediately when you play the card. An hourglass means it scores VP at the end of the game. A permanent symbol means the effect applies for the rest of the game — like reducing card costs by 1 coin. Keep played cards with ongoing or end-game effects fanned out in front of you so you remember them.

walkthrough.title: Special Projects on Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Instead of playing a card from your hand, you can complete a special project shown on one of the 4 scoring tiles on the gameboard. You pay its cost in coins and resources, then get 2 inhabitants from the supply, earn the VP shown, advance 1 space on the matching income track, and place 1 inhabitant on your color space above the tile. You can only complete each special project once per game. If it is the scoring tile you chose at setup, you earn double VP.

walkthrough.title: Explore Effect
walkthrough.text: When you explore, you reveal a gray building tile from the mound and place it face up on any empty space on the gameboard that is adjacent to an existing building tile or connected via a bridge. The space you place it on has a printed reward — coins, cards, an inhabitant, or a production site upgrade — which you immediately collect. If all spaces are already occupied, you get 2 coins instead per unplaced tile.

walkthrough.title: Develop Effect
walkthrough.text: When you develop, you take 1 inhabitant from your ranch and place it on an unoccupied building site on any building tile on the gameboard. Each building tile has 1 or 2 building sites. A building site can hold only 1 presence — either an inhabitant, a house, or a settlement. If the site shows a cost, you pay it. If it shows a reward, you collect it. Cattle pastures never hold inhabitants. This is how you establish your presence in the regions.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Effect
walkthrough.text: Upgrading has two options. Option one: upgrade an inhabitant to a house. Choose one of your inhabitants on a building site, give up 1 inhabitant from your ranch, and replace the inhabitant with a house from the topmost non-empty tier of your player board. The replaced inhabitant returns to the supply. Option two: upgrade a house to a settlement. The house must be adjacent to at least 3 presences (any player's inhabitants, houses, settlements, or cattle). Give up 2 inhabitants from your ranch, plus extras for your 3rd and 4th settlements. Replace the house with a settlement from your board — the old house is removed from the game. Building a second settlement in the same region costs 5 VP.

walkthrough.title: Tier Bonuses from Your Player Board
walkthrough.text: When you completely empty the top tier of your player board (2 houses and 1 cattle), you immediately gain the inhabitant sitting next to that tier — put it on your ranch. The same happens with the second tier (4 houses, 1 settlement, 2 cattle) and its adjacent inhabitant. These tier bonuses are huge because they give you free inhabitants and the vacated spaces generate income at every interim scoring going forward.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Pioneer
walkthrough.text: The Pioneer action lets you play or discard a card, then explore twice, and then develop once or upgrade once. The right-side effect is unique — only the other players (not you) may draw 2 cards and then play a card of any kind or discard for 2 coins. Pioneer is the main way to expand the map and claim building sites early.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Settle
walkthrough.text: Settle lets you play or discard a card, then develop once or upgrade once. Then all players in clockwise order starting with you may develop once, upgrade once, or play a card of any kind (or discard for 2 coins). This is done sequentially, not simultaneously, because placement order matters. Settle gives everyone a development or upgrade opportunity, so timing is critical.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Cattle-breeding
walkthrough.text: Cattle-breeding lets you play or discard a card, then place 1 cattle pasture on an empty gameboard space adjacent to a building tile and collect its space reward. Then all players in clockwise order may either place 1 cattle or play/discard a card. To place cattle, choose any pasture with 3 or fewer cattle. Give up 1 inhabitant for 0 existing cattle, 2 for 1 existing, 3 for 2 existing, 4 for 3 existing — plus bonus inhabitants for your 4th and 5th cattle overall. After placing, earn 2 coins per house on adjacent building tiles.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Region Scoring
walkthrough.text: Region Scoring lets you play or discard a card, then choose one of the four regions whose label reward has not yet been covered by a region marker. Cover it and take the reward. Then all players (including you) earn 1 coin per presence they have in the other three regions. This is the only action where you directly earn resources based on your board presence.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Hire
walkthrough.text: Hire lets you play or discard a card, then take 1 inhabitant from the supply to your ranch and explore once. The right-side effect lets all players pay 3 coins or discard 3 cards to take 1 inhabitant from supply to ranch, repeatable as many times as they can afford. Inhabitants are your most constrained resource, so Hire is essential for fueling upgrades and cattle placement.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Progress
walkthrough.text: Progress lets you play or discard a card, then modernize once. All other players may also modernize once, but they must give up 1 inhabitant to do so — if they cannot or will not, they may draw 1 card instead. If you choose this action, you can modernize up to twice total (once from the main effect, once from the shared effect). Modernizing means choosing an open lever space on your board, paying its coin cost, and placing a lever tile from the supply.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions: Builder
walkthrough.text: Builder is different from all other actions. Instead of playing a card at the start, you receive 3 coins. Then you may play 1 card of any kind or draw 2 cards. The right-side effect lets all players play a card of any kind or discard for 2 coins. Builder gives you the most flexibility with card types and the extra coins help fund expensive plays.

walkthrough.title: Lever Tiles and Modernization
walkthrough.text: Lever tiles go in the modernization area of your player board. Each lever has a one-time-use effect that you can trigger at any point during the game — even during another action or an interim scoring. After using a lever, push it down to show it has been spent. At each interim scoring, all levers reset to the up position so you can use them again. Unused levers at interim scoring earn you 1 VP each. At final scoring, levers earn 2 VP each if you have 4-6, or 3 VP each if you have 7 or more. The top-row levers have a special condition: you can only use them if you give up the right-side shared effect of the current action.

walkthrough.title: Interim Scoring: Five Steps
walkthrough.text: All players participate in every interim scoring. Step 1: Each player may play a card and/or upgrade once (up to 2 total actions — discarding for coins is not allowed, and developing is not allowed). Step 2: Score one of the 4 scoring tiles — place your numbered scoring marker (1/2/3/4 VP for the 1st/2nd/3rd/4th scoring) below a tile you have not scored yet. If you meet the condition, gain the VP shown; if not, lose that many VP. Your chosen tile doubles these amounts. Step 3: Collect income — coins and cards from your income track positions, plus coins and VP from vacated player board spaces, plus 1 VP per settlement adjacent to each of your cattle on the gameboard. Step 4: Reset all used levers to the up position. Earn 1 VP for each lever you did not use. Step 5: Remove all region markers from the board (resetting Region Scoring options).

walkthrough.title: End of Round and Ship Reset
walkthrough.text: After the 2nd interim scoring of a round, the round ends. If this is the end of round 1, all ships move back to their starting positions for round 2 — ships take the lower fork of the river in round 2. If this is the end of round 2, the game proceeds to the 4th interim scoring and then final scoring. Important: the 4th interim scoring does not happen immediately when a ship reaches the final space. Instead, the round continues until all players have had the same number of turns. Then conduct the scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the last interim scoring, resolve the final scoring in order. First, levers: earn 2 VP per lever if you have 4-6, or 3 VP per lever if you have 7 or more. Second, score end-game effects on all played project cards. Third, earn 2 VP per wooden piece removed from tier 3 of your player board, 4 VP per piece from tier 4, and 6 VP per piece from tier 5. Leftover coins and cards are worth nothing. The player with the most VP wins. In a tie, all tied players share the victory.

summary.title: Game Overview: Two Rounds
bullets: Game = 2 rounds, each ending when a ship reaches the last river space
2 interim scorings per round = 4 total across the game
Final scoring after the 4th interim scoring at end of round 2

summary.title: Your Turn: Three Phases
bullets: Phase A: Choose 1 action strip and carry it out
Phase B: Advance your ship along the river
Phase C: Push action strips together to close the gap

summary.title: Phase A: Choosing an Action Strip
bullets: Pull 1 of 7 action strips from the action board to the bottom
Taking a strip from the bottom 2 rows costs VP (shown on the row)
Top strips are free — tension between desired action and VP cost

summary.title: Phase A: Resolving the Action Strip
bullets: Left: play/discard a card of the shown type (or skip)
Middle: active player effects, resolved left to right
Right of arrow: shared effects for all players (or others only for Pioneer)

summary.title: Phase B: Advance Your Ship
bullets: Max steps = number on the uncovered action board space
Must move at least 1 step, never backward
Harbors hold unlimited ships (always counted); other spaces hold 1 (skip occupied)
Collect reward on landing space: coins, cards, inhabitants, vases, VP, or production upgrade

summary.title: Locks and Interim Scoring Triggers
bullets: 3 locks on the river — first ship past a lock triggers interim scoring
Second ship past the same lock does NOT trigger scoring
Round 1: upper fork; Round 2: lower fork
Ships reset between rounds

summary.title: Phase C: Close the Gap
bullets: Push remaining action strips upward to fill the empty row
The chosen strip sits at the bottom (higher VP cost next time)
Next player clockwise takes their turn

summary.title: Playing Project Cards
bullets: Cards cost coins (spent), vases (spent), and resources (shown, not spent)
Resources come from production sites (level 1 = 1, level 2 = 2) and boats
Boats: move downstream free, upstream costs 2 coins
Resource costs are virtual — you prove you have them, never lose them

summary.title: Card Types and Effects
bullets: 3 types: day, sunset, night — some actions restrict which you play
Lightning bolt = immediate effect on play
Hourglass = end-game VP scoring
Permanent symbol = ongoing effect for rest of game

summary.title: Special Projects on Scoring Tiles
bullets: Complete instead of playing a card — pay coins + resources
Gain 2 inhabitants, VP, +1 on income track, place 1 inhabitant on tile
Each project completable only once per player per game
Your chosen tile = double VP

summary.title: Explore Effect
bullets: Reveal 1 gray building tile, place on empty adjacent/bridged space
Collect space reward (coins, cards, inhabitant, or production upgrade)
All spaces full = 2 coins per unplaced tile instead

summary.title: Develop Effect
bullets: Place 1 inhabitant from ranch onto an unoccupied building site
Pay costs or collect rewards shown on the site
Cattle pastures cannot hold inhabitants
1 presence per building site maximum

summary.title: Upgrade Effect
bullets: Inhabitant to house: give up 1 ranch inhabitant, replace with house from board
House to settlement: need 3+ adjacent presences, give up 2 ranch inhabitants
3rd settlement: +1 extra inhabitant (or 4 VP); 4th: +2 extra (or 4 VP each)
2nd settlement in same region costs 5 VP

summary.title: Tier Bonuses from Your Player Board
bullets: Clear top tier (2 houses + 1 cattle) = gain tier bonus inhabitant
Clear 2nd tier (4 houses + 1 settlement + 2 cattle) = gain another inhabitant
Vacated spaces generate income at every interim scoring

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Pioneer
bullets: Play/discard card, then explore twice, then develop or upgrade once
Right side (others only): draw 2 cards, then play/discard any card
Best early action for expanding the map and claiming sites

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Settle
bullets: Play/discard card, then develop or upgrade once
Right side (all, clockwise): develop, upgrade, or play/discard any card
Sequential resolution — placement order matters

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Cattle-breeding
bullets: Play/discard card, place 1 cattle pasture and collect space reward
Right side (all, clockwise): place 1 cattle or play/discard a card
Cattle cost: give up inhabitants equal to (cattle already on pasture + 1)
Earn 2 coins per adjacent house after placing cattle

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Region Scoring
bullets: Play/discard card, choose uncovered region, take its reward
All players earn 1 coin per presence in the other 3 regions
Direct income from board presence — scales with territory

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Hire
bullets: Play/discard card, take 1 inhabitant + explore once
Right side (all): pay 3 coins or discard 3 cards per inhabitant, repeatable
Critical for fueling upgrades and cattle placement

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Progress
bullets: Play/discard card, then modernize once (place a lever)
Right side (all others): modernize once but give up 1 inhabitant, or draw 1 card
Active player can modernize up to twice total

summary.title: The Seven Actions: Builder
bullets: Unique: get 3 coins instead of playing/discarding at start
Then play any card type or draw 2 cards
Right side (all): play/discard any card type
Most flexible for card play and card economy

summary.title: Lever Tiles and Modernization
bullets: Levers go in modernization area — one-time use until reset at interim scoring
Unused levers earn 1 VP each at interim scoring
Final scoring: 2 VP each (4-6 levers) or 3 VP each (7+ levers)
Top-row levers require giving up the shared right-side action effect

summary.title: Interim Scoring: Five Steps
bullets: Step 1: Play a card and/or upgrade once (no discard-for-coins, no develop)
Step 2: Score 1 of 4 scoring tiles — gain or lose VP based on condition (x2 for your chosen tile)
Step 3: Income — track income + board income + cattle scoring
Step 4: Reset levers (1 VP per unused lever); Step 5: Remove region markers

summary.title: End of Round and Ship Reset
bullets: 2nd interim scoring ends the round — ships reset for round 2
Round 2 ships take the lower river fork
4th interim scoring: round continues until equal turns, then score
All players must have the same number of turns before final scoring

summary.title: Final Scoring
bullets: Levers: 2 VP each (4-6) or 3 VP each (7+)
End-game card effects: VP from hourglass symbols
Board tiers: 2 VP per tier-3 piece, 4 VP per tier-4, 6 VP per tier-5
Leftover coins/cards = 0 VP; ties = shared victory

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening sequence with three players — Alice, Ben, and Clara. Alice goes first, then Ben, then Clara clockwise. Each player will take different actions so you can see how the core mechanisms interact. We will also see some common mistakes that trip up new players in this heavy game. Follow along even if your player count differs. Ready? Let's settle Boonlake.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Alice has the starting player token and 6 coins. Ben has 7 coins. Clara has 8 coins. Everyone has 6 project cards in hand, 3 inhabitants on their ranch, both boats on wood, and 1 level-1 production site. The 7 action strips sit on the action board in random order. Each player has chosen a scoring tile and placed it in their color area on the gameboard. Time for the first turns of round 1.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Turn: Pioneer Action
walkthrough.text: Alice looks at the action board and finds the Pioneer strip near the top — great, no VP cost. She pulls it to the bottom. First, she plays a day card from her hand. It costs 3 coins and requires 1 wood. She has wood from her boats, so she pays the 3 coins and lays the card out. The card has a lightning effect that gives her 2 inhabitants from the supply — nice. Then she executes the middle effects: explore twice. She reveals two gray tiles and places them on adjacent empty spaces, collecting 2 coins from one space and a card draw from the other. Finally, she develops once, placing an inhabitant from her ranch on a building site that rewards her with 1 coin-track advance. After the action, Alice moves her ship 3 steps along the river and lands on a space worth 2 coins.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Strong Opening
walkthrough.text: Alice accomplished a lot in one turn. She played a card that gave her 2 extra inhabitants — critical fuel for future upgrades. She explored twice, expanding the map and collecting rewards. She developed, putting a presence on the board and advancing her income track. And her ship moved 3 steps, netting 2 coins. The Pioneer action is one of the best early-game choices because exploring is only valuable when there are still open spaces, and establishing presence early compounds through region scoring.

walkthrough.title: Ben's Turn: Hire Action
walkthrough.text: Ben needs inhabitants — he only has 3 on his ranch. He finds the Hire strip and pulls it down. He discards a sunset card for 2 coins rather than playing it, since he cannot afford its resource cost yet. Then he takes 1 free inhabitant from the supply to his ranch and explores once, placing a tile and getting a production site upgrade — he flips his loam production to level 2. Now he has 2 loam available. For the shared effect, Alice pays 3 coins to take another inhabitant. Clara also pays 3 coins for an inhabitant. Ben then moves his ship 2 steps down the river, landing on a space that lets him draw 1 card.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Benefits from Every Action
walkthrough.text: Notice how Alice and Clara both gained inhabitants from Ben's Hire action? That is a core concept in Boonlake — almost every action gives something to all players. This means you need to think about what you are giving away, not just what you are getting. The only exception is Pioneer, where the right-side effect goes to everyone except the active player. Keep this shared-benefit dynamic in mind when choosing which strip to pull.

walkthrough.title: Clara's Turn: The First Mistake
walkthrough.text: Clara sees the Builder action near the top of the board. She pulls it and collects 3 coins. Then she tries to play an expensive night card that requires 3 iron and 2 stone. Her production site is loam. Both her boats are on wood. She has zero iron and zero stone available. She cannot play this card. She forgot that resources come from production sites and boats, not from coins. She wasted her card play opportunity and now just draws 2 cards as the Builder's alternative. Her ship only moves 1 step.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Resources Are Not Coins
walkthrough.text: This is the number one mistake new players make in Boonlake. Resources are virtual — they come from production sites and boat positions on your canal. You cannot buy resources with coins. Before playing a card, always check: do my production sites and boats provide the resources this card needs? If not, you either need to move your boats (2 coins per upstream move), build more production sites, or play a different card. Plan your resource infrastructure before committing to expensive cards.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Turn: Settle
walkthrough.text: Alice pulls the Settle action. She plays a sunset card by paying its cost — 2 coins and showing 1 loam (which she can produce). The card has a permanent effect reducing future card costs by 1 coin. Then she upgrades one of her inhabitants on a building site to a house, giving up 1 inhabitant from her ranch. She removes a house from the top tier of her player board to place on the gameboard. For the shared effect, Ben develops by placing an inhabitant from his ranch onto a building site. Clara plays a day card that she can actually afford this time.

walkthrough.title: Ben's Second Turn: Cattle-breeding
walkthrough.text: Ben pulls the Cattle-breeding strip. He discards a card for 2 coins. Then he places a cattle pasture from the supply onto an empty gameboard space adjacent to a building tile and collects the space reward of 3 coins. For the shared effect, Ben places cattle first. He chooses a pasture with 0 cattle, giving up 1 inhabitant from his ranch. He takes the topmost cattle from his player board. Then he earns 2 coins for each house adjacent to that pasture — there is 1 house there (Alice's), so he gets 2 coins. Alice places cattle too, giving up 1 inhabitant. Clara discards a card for 2 coins instead.

walkthrough.title: Clara's Second Turn: The Second Mistake
walkthrough.text: Clara wants to settle — she pulls the Settle action. She develops, placing an inhabitant on a building site. Then on the shared effect, she wants to upgrade that same inhabitant to a house immediately. But she only has 1 inhabitant left on her ranch. Upgrading to a house costs 1 ranch inhabitant, and she needs that inhabitant. She is stuck with no inhabitants on her ranch, which means she cannot upgrade, cannot place cattle, and cannot fuel any future effects that require giving up inhabitants. She should have used the Hire action earlier to build up her ranch.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Inhabitants Are Your Most Critical Resource
walkthrough.text: Inhabitants fuel everything in Boonlake. You need them to develop, to upgrade to houses, to upgrade to settlements, and to place cattle. Running out of ranch inhabitants is devastating — it freezes your entire engine. Always keep at least 2-3 inhabitants on your ranch as a buffer. Use the Hire action regularly. Pay 3 coins or discard 3 cards on other players' Hire turns to restock. Never spend your last inhabitants without a plan to get more.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Third Turn: Progress
walkthrough.text: Alice pulls the Progress strip and modernizes. She pays 4 coins and places a lever tile in her modernization area. She chooses a lever that lets her reduce card resource costs by 1 whenever she plays a card. This is a powerful permanent-like ability that recharges every interim scoring. For the shared effect, Ben decides to give up 1 inhabitant to modernize. Clara cannot give up an inhabitant (she has none on her ranch), so she draws 1 card instead.

walkthrough.title: Why Levers Are So Valuable
walkthrough.text: Alice's lever is a repeating resource discount — she can use it once per interim scoring period to reduce a card's resource cost by 1. That might not sound like much, but it means she can play cards she otherwise could not afford, and it refreshes four times over the game. Levers also score 1 VP each when unused at interim scoring, and 2-3 VP each at final scoring. Getting levers early maximizes their value because you get more uses out of them.

walkthrough.title: Ben's Third Turn: Region Scoring
walkthrough.text: Ben pulls the Region Scoring strip. He chooses the Boonlake region (not yet covered) and places the region marker on its reward — 4 coins. He takes 4 coins. Then all players count their presences in the other three regions. Ben has 2 presences and earns 2 coins. Alice has 3 presences and earns 3 coins. Clara has 1 presence and earns 1 coin. Region scoring rewards everyone who has spread out across the map.

walkthrough.title: Clara's Mistake: Ignoring Ship Movement
walkthrough.text: Clara has been so focused on the action effects that she has only moved her ship 3 total spaces across her first two turns. Alice's ship is already 8 spaces ahead. If Alice reaches a lock first, she triggers the interim scoring — and Clara has not had time to set up for her scoring tile condition. Clara also missed valuable river rewards — free coins, cards, inhabitants, and production site upgrades from landing on good spaces. Her ship position directly controls how many steps she can take, and she has been ignoring it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Ship Movement Is Not Optional Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your ship movement is mandatory — at least 1 step per turn — and it controls the game's tempo. The first player to cross a lock triggers interim scoring for everyone. If you fall behind, you will not be prepared for scoring tile conditions and you will miss income phases when you need them most. Also, river space rewards are free resources. Plan your action strip choice partly based on how far it lets your ship move. Balancing action quality with movement range is key to good play.

walkthrough.title: First Lock Approaches
walkthrough.text: Play continues for a few more turns. Alice has been advancing steadily and her ship is about to cross the first lock. When she does, the first interim scoring triggers immediately for all players. Alice has been building her income tracks, placing presences in regions, and removing pieces from her player board. Ben has solid cattle income set up. Clara is scrambling — she has low income, few presences, and she is about to face her first scoring tile check unprepared.

walkthrough.title: First Interim Scoring Walkthrough
walkthrough.text: Alice crosses the lock and collects her river reward. Now interim scoring begins. Step 1: everyone may play a card and/or upgrade once. Alice upgrades a house to a settlement, using her well-stocked ranch. Ben plays a card. Clara has no inhabitants to upgrade with, so she plays a card she can barely afford. Step 2: everyone scores a scoring tile. Alice scores her x2 tile and meets the condition — big VP gain. Ben meets his condition too. Clara cannot meet her chosen tile's condition and must subtract VP. Step 3: income — everyone collects coins, cards, and VP from their income tracks and board. Step 4: reset levers and earn 1 VP per unused lever. Step 5: remove region markers.

walkthrough.title: Clara's Recovery Plan
walkthrough.text: Clara is behind, but this game is long — there are 3 more interim scorings and a full final scoring ahead. Her priorities now: first, use the Hire action aggressively to rebuild her ranch. Second, move boats to get the resources she needs for her cards. Third, get her ship moving to avoid being caught off-guard at the next lock. Fourth, focus on building presences in regions where her scoring tile conditions need them. Boonlake rewards adaptation — you can recover from a rough start if you identify the bottleneck and fix it.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Round
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player pulling action strips, carrying out effects, and advancing ships. The second lock is further down the river and will trigger the second interim scoring. After that, round 1 ends and ships reset. Round 2 follows the same structure with 2 more interim scorings and then the final scoring. As you play, notice how the shared action effects create an interconnected economy — choosing when to give opponents useful actions versus when to take what you need is the heart of Boonlake's strategy.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all three turn phases, watched the core actions in practice, experienced your first interim scoring, and learned from three critical mistakes. Remember: resources are not coins — check your production sites and boats. Inhabitants are your most precious resource — never run dry. And ship movement controls the game's pace — do not ignore it. You are ready to play Boonlake on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore Further
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals and more common pitfalls to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every action, effect, building site, and scoring condition.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun settling Boonlake!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before strategy, let's cover the traps that catch new Boonlake players. First, trying to play cards without the resources — remember, resources come from production sites and boats, not coins. Second, running out of inhabitants on your ranch — inhabitants fuel everything and running dry freezes your engine for multiple turns. Third, ignoring ship movement and getting caught unprepared by interim scoring. Fourth, picking an action strip purely for its action while ignoring the VP cost of bottom rows and the movement range it provides. Fifth, forgetting that almost every action benefits all players — you are always giving something to opponents. Sixth, neglecting the income tracks — each step on the coin or card track pays off at every interim scoring for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Opening Priorities
walkthrough.text: In the first few turns of round 1, focus on three things. First, get your resource infrastructure set up — build or upgrade a production site if you can, and position your boats to match the cards you want to play. Second, establish early presence on the gameboard through developing and exploring — presence generates coins at Region Scoring and feeds your scoring tile conditions. Third, start building your inhabitant supply through the Hire action. A good opening plays one or two cheap cards, places a few presences on the board, and keeps at least 2 inhabitants on the ranch at all times.

walkthrough.title: Income Track Advancement
walkthrough.text: The coin track and card track are deceptively powerful. Each step you advance on these tracks increases the income you receive at every future interim scoring. Getting to position 4 or 5 on the coin track by the first interim scoring means you will collect that income three more times across the game. Early income track advancement has a massive compounding effect. Look for cards and building site effects that advance your markers. The income track also has milestone rewards you collect immediately when your marker passes them — including production site upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Managing Your Inhabitant Economy
walkthrough.text: Inhabitants are Boonlake's most constrained resource. You start with only 3 on your ranch and 5 in the supply. You spend inhabitants to develop, to upgrade to houses (1 inhabitant), to upgrade to settlements (2 plus extras for 3rd and 4th), and to place cattle (1 per existing cattle on the pasture plus extras for 4th and 5th overall). The Hire action is the main way to replenish — either as the active player or by paying 3 coins or discarding 3 cards on someone else's Hire turn. Also look for card effects that give inhabitants. Always plan your inhabitant spending two turns ahead.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Tile Strategy
walkthrough.text: Scoring tiles are scored four times — once per interim scoring — with increasingly difficult conditions. Failing a condition costs you the VP instead of gaining them, and your personal tile doubles both gains and losses. This means your chosen tile can swing 30 or more VP across the game. Pick your scoring tile based on what you can realistically build toward with your starting hand. Study the four conditions before the game starts and plan a path to meet each one. The first condition is usually easy — the fourth is very demanding. It is fine to intentionally fail the hardest condition on another player's tile to save resources, but never fail your own double tile if you can help it.

walkthrough.title: Ship Movement and Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: Your ship does two critical things. It provides river rewards (free coins, cards, inhabitants, vases, VP, and production site upgrades) and it controls the pace of the game. The first player to cross a lock triggers interim scoring for everyone. If you are ahead on the river, you can force scoring when you are ready and opponents are not. If you are behind, you may get caught off-guard. When choosing action strips, weigh the movement range alongside the action itself — sometimes a weaker action with more movement is the better choice. Also note that harbors do not block movement, but regular spaces occupied by other ships are skipped without counting.

walkthrough.title: Boat Positioning and Resource Planning
walkthrough.text: Your two canal boats each provide 1 resource. Moving downstream is free, but moving upstream costs 2 coins per boat. This means shifting to a cheaper resource (further downstream) is easy, but accessing expensive resources upstream requires coins. Plan your boat positions at the start of each turn cycle. If you know you want to play a card requiring iron next turn, move a boat to iron now while you have the coins. Do not wait until the moment you need to play the card — you might not have the 2 coins to spare when it matters.

walkthrough.title: Timing Shared Effects to Your Advantage
walkthrough.text: Almost every action lets all players do something on the right side of the strip. Think about when to give opponents useful effects and when to deny them. If everyone needs inhabitants, choosing Hire helps everyone — but maybe you need it more. If you choose Region Scoring when opponents have more presences than you, they profit more. The Pioneer is special — the right side helps only opponents, so use it when the draw-and-play benefit is low for them. Settle and Cattle-breeding are sequential clockwise, so going first gives you priority on the best building sites and pastures.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Resources ≠ coins — they come from production sites and boats
Never run out of ranch inhabitants — it freezes your engine
Don't ignore ship movement — it controls scoring tempo and gives free rewards
Consider VP cost of bottom action strips and what you give opponents

summary.title: Opening Priorities
bullets: Set up resource infrastructure (production sites + boat positions)
Establish early board presence through developing and exploring
Build inhabitant supply via Hire
Keep 2+ inhabitants on ranch at all times

summary.title: Income Track Advancement

Reaching position 4-5 by first scoring = 3 more payoffs across the game
Milestone rewards (including production upgrades) trigger immediately
Compounding effect makes early track advancement very high value

summary.title: Managing Your Inhabitant Economy
bullets: Start with 3 ranch + 5 supply = 8 total accessible inhabitants
Spent on: developing, upgrading (houses/settlements), placing cattle
Restock via Hire action (active or shared) and card effects
Plan inhabitant spending 2 turns ahead — never run dry

summary.title: Scoring Tile Strategy
bullets: 4 scoring checks with escalating difficulty — your tile is doubled (gains AND losses)
Failing costs VP — failing your own double tile is devastating
Pick tile matching your starting hand and achievable conditions
OK to intentionally fail someone else's tile to save resources

summary.title: Ship Movement and Tempo Control
bullets: River rewards are free resources — don't leave them on the table
First past a lock triggers interim scoring — be ready or be ahead
Weigh movement range alongside action quality when choosing strips
Harbors don't block; occupied regular spaces are skipped (not counted)

summary.title: Boat Positioning and Resource Planning
bullets: Each boat = 1 resource at its canal position
Downstream free, upstream costs 2 coins — plan moves ahead of time
Don't wait until you need the resource — position boats proactively
Level-2 production sites give 2 resources — reduce boat dependency

summary.title: Timing Shared Effects to Your Advantage
bullets: Almost every action benefits all players — consider what you give away
Pioneer right-side only helps opponents — use when draw/play value is low for them
Settle/Cattle-breeding are sequential — going first = priority on best sites
Region Scoring helps players with more presences — timing matters

walkthrough.title: Settlement Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The settlement rush aims to clear your player board quickly by upgrading to houses and then settlements as fast as possible. Each cleared tier unlocks permanent income at interim scoring and eventually yields bonus inhabitants from tier bonuses. Settlements also unlock the largest board spaces for final scoring — tier 3, 4, and 5 pieces are worth 2, 4, and 6 VP each at final scoring. The key is maintaining a robust inhabitant pipeline, because settlements are expensive: 2 inhabitants base, plus extra for your 3rd and 4th. Pair this with frequent Hire actions and cards that give free inhabitants.

walkthrough.title: Building the Settlement Engine
walkthrough.text: Start by developing aggressively in regions with dense building tiles — you need 3 adjacent presences before upgrading a house to a settlement. Other players' presences count, so develop near their positions. Use the Settle action as both active and shared effect to chain develop-upgrade sequences. Watch out for the 5 VP penalty for building a second settlement in the same region — spread across regions to avoid this. Aim for 3 settlements by the third interim scoring and possibly a 4th by the end of the game. Each settlement clears board space and generates cattle scoring adjacency.

walkthrough.title: Cattle-Focused Strategy
walkthrough.text: Cattle placement earns immediate coins from adjacent houses and 1 VP per adjacent settlement at each interim scoring. The cattle income is recurring and grows as more settlements appear near pastures. Target pastures near clusters of building sites where settlements will be built. Cattle also clear your player board, unlocking tier income. The downside is the escalating inhabitant cost — your 4th and 5th cattle cost extra inhabitants. Cards with permanent effects that reduce cattle placement costs or give extra coins when placing cattle are extremely valuable in this approach.

walkthrough.title: Lever Accumulation Strategy
walkthrough.text: Levers provide three types of value: powerful one-time-per-scoring-period effects, 1 VP each when unused at interim scoring, and 2-3 VP each at final scoring. A player with 7 levers earns 21 VP at final scoring alone, plus has gotten significant utility from their effects all game. The leap from 6 to 7 levers changes the per-lever value from 2 to 3 VP, making that 7th lever worth 7 extra VP at endgame (21 vs 12). Focus on the Progress action and cards that reduce lever costs. The top-row levers that require forgoing the shared action effect are especially strong because they give unique abilities like free boat movement, extra upgrades, and vases.

walkthrough.title: Card Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: A card-focused strategy leverages the Builder action, card-drawing river spaces, and the card income track to maintain a large hand and play high-value cards every turn. Permanent effects from played cards compound — cost reduction, extra coins when discarding, extra vases, and cattle placement discounts all stack. Focus on advancing the card income track quickly to draw more cards at each interim scoring. The Builder action is your bread and butter: it gives 3 coins and lets you play any card type. Look for end-game scoring cards early and play them when you can afford them.

walkthrough.title: Region Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: Controlling regions pays off through three mechanisms: Region Scoring income (1 coin per presence in scored regions), scoring tile conditions that require presences in specific regions, and end-game card effects that award VP per presence in a named region. Focus your development in 2-3 adjacent regions. Build houses and settlements there to count as higher-value presences for scoring tiles. Use Region Scoring strategically — choose the region that gives you the best reward while denying the best option to opponents. The region markers reset at each interim scoring, so every region can be scored multiple times.

walkthrough.title: Income Track Rushing
walkthrough.text: Aggressively advancing both income tracks is one of the most consistent strategies. Each income track step pays dividends at every remaining interim scoring. If you can reach position 6 on the coin track by the second interim scoring, you collect that income twice more — a massive coin advantage. The income tracks also have milestone rewards along the way, including production site upgrades that expand your resource access. Look for cards and building site effects that advance income markers. Some building sites give an immediate income collection effect (get your current income without advancing the marker), which is powerful when your marker is already high.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Tile Maximization
walkthrough.text: Your personal double-scoring tile is the single highest VP-swing element in the game. At the 4th interim scoring, your marker is worth 4 VP — doubled to 8 for meeting the condition on your tile, or minus 8 for failing. That is a 16 VP swing in one check. Study your tile's four conditions at the start and build your entire game plan to meet them. Conditions that require income track progress, lever counts, or specific board removals can be planned well ahead. Conditions requiring presences in specific regions need early map development. If a condition requires many cattle removals or settlement placements, prioritize those tracks. Plan to meet the third and fourth conditions even if they require sacrificing other scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Special Project Timing
walkthrough.text: The special projects on scoring tiles are expensive but can be worth it — especially your own double-VP project. Completing your own special project early maximizes its benefit: you get double VP, advance an income track (which pays off at future scorings), and gain 2 inhabitants. The best time to complete a project is just before an interim scoring, so you can immediately benefit from the income track advance and use the inhabitants you gained. Watch for opponents completing their projects too — once a scoring tile project is done, it cannot be done again, but any player can complete any tile's project (at normal VP, not doubled).

walkthrough.title: Endgame Pivot and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: In the second half of round 2, shift focus to final scoring categories. Count your levers — if you are at 6, that 7th lever is worth a huge jump in per-lever VP. Check your board — which tier 3/4/5 pieces can you still remove? Each tier-5 piece is worth 6 VP, so removing even one more can swing the game. Make sure all your end-game card effects are maximized — VP-per-presence cards reward having developed broadly. And remember: at the 4th interim scoring, coins and cards from income are worthless (only VP matters). So in the final turns, do not invest in coin or card track advancement — invest in VP-generating plays instead.

summary.title: Settlement Rush Strategy
bullets: Clear player board quickly — tier income + tier-3/4/5 final scoring VP
Settlements unlock the highest VP spaces (2/4/6 VP per tier piece)
Requires robust inhabitant pipeline — pair with frequent Hire
Tier bonuses give free inhabitants, accelerating the engine

summary.title: Building the Settlement Engine
bullets: Develop near dense building tiles — need 3 adjacent presences for settlements
Other players' presences count toward the adjacency requirement
Spread across regions to avoid the 5 VP same-region penalty
Aim for 3 settlements by 3rd interim scoring

summary.title: Cattle-Focused Strategy
bullets: Cattle earn immediate coins (adjacent houses) + recurring VP (adjacent settlements)
Target pastures near building site clusters for maximum adjacency
Clears player board for tier income — compounds with settlement strategy
Cards reducing cattle inhabitant costs are extremely valuable

summary.title: Lever Accumulation Strategy
bullets: 7 levers = 21 VP at final scoring (3 VP each); 6 levers = only 12 VP (2 VP each)
The 7th lever is worth 9 extra VP over the 6th — a massive threshold
Levers also provide powerful repeating effects that reset at interim scoring
Top-row levers (sacrifice shared action) offer unique abilities

summary.title: Card Engine Strategy
bullets: Builder action is core: 3 coins + play any card type
Permanent card effects stack and compound throughout the game
Card income track ensures a large hand for consistent plays
End-game scoring cards are best played early to plan around

summary.title: Region Dominance Strategy
bullets: Region Scoring: 1 coin per presence in scored regions — scales with development
Focus 2-3 adjacent regions for concentrated scoring tile fulfillment
End-game cards awarding VP per presence in a region reward broad development
Region markers reset at interim scoring — regions can be scored multiple times

summary.title: Income Track Rushing
bullets: Each track step pays off at every remaining interim scoring — compound returns
Milestone rewards include production site upgrades
Building sites with 'get current income' effects are very powerful at high track positions
Consistent strategy that synergizes with any other approach

summary.title: Scoring Tile Maximization
bullets: 4th scoring check on your double tile: 16 VP swing (8 gain vs 8 loss)
Study all 4 conditions at game start and plan your entire game around them
Sacrifice other scoring if needed to meet your tile's hardest conditions
Most impactful single element in the game

summary.title: Special Project Timing
bullets: Complete your double-VP project early for maximum income track compound
Best timed just before interim scoring to immediately benefit from the track advance
Grants 2 inhabitants — valuable fuel for immediate upgrades
Any player can complete any tile's project (normal VP), only yours is doubled

summary.title: Endgame Pivot and Final Scoring
bullets: 7th lever threshold: massive VP jump — prioritize if at 6 levers
Tier-5 removals worth 6 VP each — push for any remaining pieces
4th interim scoring: only VP matters (coins/cards go to waste)
End-game card effects reward broad development — maximize before final scoring

entries.definition: One of 7 strips placed on the action board. On your turn, you pull one strip to the bottom, carry out its effects, and advance your ship. Each strip shows a unique action with left (card play), middle (active player effects), and right (shared effects) sections.
The tall narrow board that holds the 7 action strips in a vertical stack. The row a strip is taken from determines the VP cost (bottom 2 rows cost VP) and the maximum ship movement for that turn.
The first phase of your turn. Pull one action strip from the action board and resolve its effects: card play, middle effects (active player), and right-side effects (all or other players).
The second phase. Move your ship down the river up to the number of steps shown on the uncovered space. Must move at least 1 step. Collect the reward on your landing space.
The third phase. Push the remaining action strips upward on the action board to fill the empty row. Each row now has exactly one strip again.

Action: Play/discard a card, then explore twice, then develop or upgrade once. Right side (others only): draw 2 cards, then play/discard any card. The only action where the active player is excluded from the shared effect.
Action: Play/discard a card, then develop or upgrade once. Right side (all, clockwise): develop, upgrade, or play/discard any card. Sequential resolution — placement order matters.

Action: Play/discard a card, place 1 cattle pasture on an empty adjacent space and collect its reward. Right side (all, clockwise): place 1 cattle or play/discard a card. Cattle cost escalates with pasture occupancy.
Action: Play/discard a card, choose 1 uncovered region and take its label reward (place region marker). Then all players earn 1 coin per presence in the other 3 regions.

Action: Play/discard a card, take 1 inhabitant from supply + explore once. Right side (all): pay 3 coins or discard 3 cards per inhabitant, repeatable. Cannot mix coins and cards for a single inhabitant.
Action: Play/discard a card, then modernize once. Right side (all others): modernize once (give up 1 inhabitant) or draw 1 card. Active player can modernize up to twice total.
Action: Receive 3 coins (instead of card play/discard), then play any card type or draw 2 cards. Right side (all): play/discard any card. Most flexible action for card economy.
Reveal a gray building tile from the mound and place it face up on any empty gameboard space adjacent to an existing building tile (or connected via bridge). Collect the space's printed reward.

Place 1 inhabitant from your ranch onto an unoccupied building site on any building tile on the gameboard. Pay any costs or collect any rewards shown on the site. Cattle pastures cannot hold inhabitants.
Choose your inhabitant on a building site, give up 1 ranch inhabitant, replace the inhabitant with a house from the topmost non-empty tier of your player board. Replaced inhabitant returns to supply.

Choose your house adjacent to 3+ presences (any player's). Give up 2 ranch inhabitants (+1 extra for 3rd settlement, +2 for 4th — or 4 VP per missing inhabitant). Replace house with settlement from board. Removed house is out of game. 2nd settlement in same region costs 5 VP.
Choose an open lever space on your player board, pay the coin cost shown, and place a lever tile from the supply. The lever provides a one-time-use effect that resets at each interim scoring.

Lay a project card from your hand in front of you after paying its cost in coins, vases, and resources. Resources are proven by production sites and boats (not spent). Some effects restrict card type (day/sunset/night).
Instead of playing a card, discard any card of the required type to gain 2 coins (or 3 coins with the Builder action). The card goes to the discard pile.
One of 165 cards with costs (coins, vases, resources) and effects (immediate, end-game, or permanent). Three types: day, sunset, and night. Played cards stay in your display for the rest of the game.
One of the three project card types, identified by a sun symbol. Some action strips restrict card play to specific types.
One of the three project card types, identified by a sunset symbol. Some action strips restrict card play to specific types.
One of the three project card types, identified by a moon symbol. Some action strips restrict card play to specific types.
Card effect that triggers once when the card is played. Shown by a lightning bolt symbol. Examples: gain coins, take inhabitants, advance income track, take a vase.
Card effect that scores VP at final scoring. Shown by an hourglass symbol. Examples: VP per presence in a region, VP per cattle removed, VP per production site level.
Card effect that applies for the rest of the game after being played. Examples: reduce card coin costs by 1, extra coins when discarding specific card types, reduced cattle placement costs.

Primary currency in Boonlake. Used to pay card costs, lever costs, building site costs, and upstream boat movement (2 coins). 62 total in supply (41x1, 15x5, 6x20). Leftover coins are worth 0 VP at end of game.
A premium resource used in card costs and some lever effects. 14 total in supply. Some river spaces and card effects grant vases. Vases are spent when used, unlike production resources.

Placed in one of 4 recesses on your player board (stone, iron, loam, wood). Level 1 provides 1 resource; level 2 provides 2. Resources are virtual — shown for card costs, never spent. Upgraded by flipping to the level-2 side.
One of 4 resource types. Produced by stone production sites and boats positioned at stone on the canal. Required by various project cards.
One of 4 resource types. Produced by iron production sites and boats positioned at iron on the canal. Required by various project cards.
One of 4 resource types. Produced by loam production sites and boats positioned at loam on the canal. Required by various project cards.
One of 4 resource types. Produced by wood production sites and boats positioned at wood on the canal. Both boats start on wood. Required by various project cards.

Each player has 2 boats on their player board canal. Each provides 1 resource matching its current canal position. Move downstream (right) for free, upstream (left) for 2 coins per boat. Key to meeting card resource requirements.

Wooden worker piece in your color. Placed on your ranch, then spent to develop (place on building sites), upgrade, and place cattle. 10 per player total — 3 start on ranch, 2 in tier bonus recesses, 5 in supply. Your most constrained resource.

The area at the bottom left of your player board where inhabitants are collected. All effects that give up inhabitants take them from the ranch. All effects that grant inhabitants place them on the ranch.

Upgraded form of an inhabitant on a building site. Placed by removing a house piece from your player board, which unlocks income at interim scorings. 6 per player. Houses removed during settlement upgrades are out of the game.

Upgraded form of a house. Requires 3+ adjacent presences to build. 4 per player maximum. Generates VP during cattle scoring at interim scorings (1 VP per adjacent cattle). Building a 2nd settlement in the same region costs 5 VP.

Placed on cattle pastures from your player board. Costs inhabitants based on pasture occupancy. Earns 2 coins per adjacent house when placed. Generates 1 VP per adjacent settlement at each interim scoring. 5 per player.

Special building tile (double-sided) placed on empty gameboard spaces during the Cattle-breeding action. Holds up to 4 cattle total from any combination of players. Cannot hold inhabitants. 7 total in supply.

Tiles placed on the gameboard during explore effects or setup. Each has 1-2 building sites where players can develop. Sites may show costs (coins/cards) or rewards (coins/cards/VP/income track/vases).
A space on a building tile where one presence (inhabitant, house, or settlement) can be placed. Some sites have costs to place on; others give rewards. Each site holds exactly 1 presence maximum.

Any of your pieces on the gameboard: inhabitants, houses, settlements, or cattle. Presences count for Region Scoring income, settlement adjacency requirements, scoring tile conditions, and end-game card effects.
One of 4 named areas on the gameboard connected by bridges. Boonlake is the northern region. Its region scoring reward is 4 coins.
One of 4 named areas on the gameboard. The southern region. Region scoring reward is typically 3 coins/cards/VP.
One of 4 named areas on the gameboard. Region scoring reward is typically 3 coins/cards/VP.
One of 4 named areas on the gameboard. Region scoring reward is typically 3 coins/cards/VP.
Connections between the 4 regions on the gameboard. Building tiles on either side of a bridge are considered adjacent. Presences next to bridges may fulfill specific scoring tile conditions.
The path along the right side of the gameboard where ships move. Contains reward spaces, harbors, locks, and a fork. Round 1 uses the upper fork; round 2 uses the lower fork.

Each player's ship moves down the river during Phase B. Movement range equals the number on the uncovered action board space. Must move at least 1 step, never backward. Collect reward on landing space.
Special river spaces that can hold any number of ships simultaneously. Always counted toward movement (unlike occupied regular spaces which are skipped). You do not have to stop at a harbor.

Three marked positions on the river. The first ship to cross a lock in a round triggers an interim scoring immediately. Locks are not counted as spaces. A second ship crossing the same lock does not trigger scoring.

One of 2 income tracks on the gameboard. Advancing earns milestone rewards and increases coin income at each interim scoring. If your marker reaches the end and would advance further, earn 2 VP per forfeited step.

One of 2 income tracks on the gameboard. Advancing earns milestone rewards and increases card draws at each interim scoring. Some positions also grant VP income. Same overflow rule as coin track (2 VP per forfeited step).

The scoring track on the gameboard. Your marker moves forward and backward throughout the game. Can go below 0. When you complete the full track, place your 100-marker on your board; second time, flip to 200.

Placed in modernization area recesses on your player board. Each has a one-time-use effect. Push down when used; reset to up at interim scoring. Unused levers earn 1 VP at interim scoring. Worth 2 VP each (4-6 total) or 3 VP each (7+) at final scoring.

One of 3 lever spaces in the top row of the modernization area. Can only be activated if you give up the shared right-side effect of the current action strip. If you have multiple, can only use 1 per action.
Lever effect that reduces a card's resource cost by 1 resource of your choice when playing it. Some versions also grant bonus coins. Can be used for special projects on scoring tiles.
Lever effect that lets you move 1 boat upstream any number of steps without paying 2 coins. Saves coins and enables resource flexibility at critical moments.
Lever effect that reduces both the inhabitant cost and the adjacency requirement for upgrading a house to a settlement by 1. Cumulative with card effects that do the same.
Lever effect that gives you 1 inhabitant when you place a building tile (including cattle pastures) on the gameboard.
Lever effect that gives you 1 coin per production site on your board when you voluntarily forgo playing a card. Level-2 sites count double.
Lever effect that gives you 3 coins when you voluntarily forgo playing a card. Works at Builder action and interim scoring card plays too.
Lever effect that gives you 1 VP per presence (in addition to the normal 1 coin) during the shared phase of Region Scoring.

16 double-purpose tiles. Upper half shows a special project (costs + rewards). Lower half shows a scoring condition checked at each of the 4 interim scorings with escalating difficulty. Your chosen tile doubles VP gains and losses.

The upper portion of a scoring tile. Completed instead of playing a card by paying coins + resources. Rewards: 2 inhabitants, VP, +1 income track, place 1 inhabitant on the tile. Once per player per game. Counts as all 3 card types.
4 per player, numbered 1-4. Placed below scoring tiles at the 1st/2nd/3rd/4th interim scorings respectively. Each marker has its own VP value. Cannot score the same tile twice.

Triggered when the first ship crosses a lock in the current round. 5 steps: (1) play card and/or upgrade, (2) score a scoring tile, (3) collect income, (4) reset levers + 1 VP per unused, (5) remove region markers. 4 total across the game.

Step 3 of interim scoring. Collect coin/card income from track positions, coins and VP from vacated player board spaces, and 1 VP per settlement adjacent to each of your cattle. At the 4th scoring, only VP is collected (coins/cards wasted).

Clearing all pieces from a tier of your player board grants the adjacent inhabitant to your ranch immediately. Tier 1: 2 houses + 1 cattle. Tier 2: 4 houses + 1 settlement + 2 cattle. Vacated spaces generate income at each interim scoring.

After the 4th interim scoring at end of round 2. Score levers (2 VP each if 4-6, or 3 VP each if 7+), end-game card effects, and board tier removals (2/4/6 VP per piece from tiers 3/4/5). Leftover coins and cards = 0 VP. Highest VP wins; ties shared.
4 markers placed on region labels. When a region is chosen during Region Scoring, its marker covers the reward. All region markers are removed (reset) at step 5 of each interim scoring.

Given to the first player. The starting player gets 6 coins; subsequent players get 7 and 8 coins. The round end condition checks that all players have had equal turns before the final interim scoring.
Placed on your player board when your VP marker completes a full lap of the VP track. Flip to 200 on the second lap. Tracks your total VP beyond what the circular track can show.
A feature on the gameboard where spaces on either side are considered adjacent for building tile placement and presence adjacency purposes, even though they are not directly next to each other.

The waterway on your player board where your 2 boats sit. Positions along the canal correspond to resource types (wood, loam, iron, stone). Boats produce 1 resource each based on their current position.

Section of your player board with spaces for lever tiles. Has a top row (3 spaces with special activation conditions) and lower rows. Each space has a coin cost. Fill spaces to gain lever effects and end-game VP.
